content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

March 25, 1997

Richard Doyle
City Attorney
City of Concord
1950 Parkside Drive, M/S 08
Concord, California 94519-2578

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-071

Dear Mr. Doyle:

This letter is a response to your request for advice on behalf of Concord City Councilmember Michael Patrick regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Is State Senator Dan Boatwright a source of income to Councilmember Michael Pastrick if the councilmember received income from the senator's controlled campaign committee within the previous 12 months?

2. If Senator Boatwright is a source of income to Councilmember Patrick, should the councilmember disqualify himself from a city council decision concerning a party which the senator will represent before the city council?

CONCLUSION

1. Yes, Senator Boatwright is a source of income to Councilmember Patrick because the Act treats a candidate and his or her controlled committee as one person for conflict-of-interest purposes.

2. Councilmember Patrick must disqualify himself from the decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Senator Boatwright. The financial effect of the decision upon Senator Boatwright is reasonably foreseeable if the senator's fees are contingent upon the outcome of the city's decision.

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-071
Page 2

FACTS

Michael Pastrick is a councilmember for the City of Concord. Beginning in 1981, he
served as treasurer and provided various other campaign services as a paid consultant for
campaign committees controlled by retired State Senator Dan Boatwright. Most recently he
served as a treasurer and consultant for the Boatwright Senate 92 Committee ("Campaign
Committee"). Senator Boatwright retired from the State Senate in December 1996, and the
Campaign Committee has since been terminated. At all times during its existence, the Campaign
Committee was controlled by Senator Boatwright and Councilmember Pastrick served as a
treasurer and as a consultant at the pleasure of the senator. Councilmember Pastrick has received
more than $250 in income from the Campaign Committee during the past 12 months.

Senator Boatwright has recently been hired as an attorney to represent Concord Disposal
Service in a rate setting and franchise extension matter currently before the Concord City
Council. Concord Disposal Service has the exclusive franchise to haul garbage in the City of
Concord. Every three years, the Concord Disposal Service is required to apply to the city council
for a revision of current rates. Concord Disposal has also requested a 13-year extension to its
franchise from the city council. You would like to know whether Councilmember Pastrick is
disqualified from participating in city decisions concerning Concord Disposal's application for
new garbage rates and/or franchise extension.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or using
their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to
know they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of
Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of
the official's immediate family, or on:

"(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than
loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made."
(Section 87103(c).)

The Campaign Committee has been source of income to Councilmember Pastrick of $250
or more within the previous 12 months. At the time the councilmember received income from
the Campaign Committee, Senator Boatwright controlled the Campaign Committee. Senator
Boatwright will be representing a party before the Concord City Council. You would like to
know whether Senator Boatwright is considered a source of income to Councilmember Pastrick
for conflict-of-interest purposes.

File No. A-97-071
Page 3

In the past, for conflict-of-interest purposes, the Commission has "pierced" through
entities to consider the nature of the relationship between a person and the entity.2 For example,
in In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6, the Commission, in its conflict-of-interest analysis, pierced
through the veil of a limited partnership and determined that the controlling general partner was
one with the limited partnership. The Commission reasoned that a limited partnership involves
the entrepreneurial skills of the general partner who has the sole discretion and authority to manage the investment when conducting the partnership
business.

The same rationale applies to candidates and their controlled committees. Section 82016
defines a "controlled committee" as the following:

"[A] committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by a
candidate or state measure proponent or which acts jointly with a
candidate, controlled committee or state measure proponent in
connection with the making of expenditures. A candidate or state
measure proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or any
other committee he controls has significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the committee." (Emphasis added.)

As expressed in the above definition, a candidate has significant discretion in connection with
the making of expenditures by his or her controlled committee. (Section 82016.) Accordingly,
we will treat a candidate and his or her controlled committee as one person for purposes of the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. Thus, Senator Boatwright is a disqualifying source of
income to Councilmember Pastrick and the councilmemer must not make, participate in making
or attempt to influence a city decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect upon the senator.

Foreseeability

You want to know whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a city decision concerning a
party represented by Senator Boatwright will financially affect the senator. This is a factual
determination which Councilmember Pastrick must make, however, we can offer the following
guidelines. Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the
time a governmental decision is made depends upon the facts of each particular case. An effect
is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) For example, it is
substantially likely that Senator Boatwright will be financially affected by the decision if his fees
are contingent upon the outcome of the decision.

2 See, for example, In re Lumsdon (1975) 2 FPPC Ops. 140 and In re Kahn (1975) 2 FPPC Ops. 151
where the Commission pierced through the corporate veil and treated the controlling shareholder as one with his
closely-held corporation.

File No. A-97-071
Page 4

Materiality

If Councilmember Pastrick finds that the financial effect of the city's decision upon
Senator Boatwright is reasonably foreseeable, the councilmember must then determine whether
the financial effect is material. The Commission has adopted a series of regulations that provide
guidance regarding whether the financial effects of a decision are material. These regulations
apply differing standards depending on whether the decision will directly³ or indirectly affect the
official's financial interest. For an individual who is a source of income to an official, the
indirect effect of a decision is material if:

"(a) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or

(b) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in
a manner that is considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4." (Regulation 18702.6.)

To determine whether the effect of the city decision on Senator Boatwright will be
material, Councilmember Pastrick must assess the potential effect of the decision by applying the
standards set forth above.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Julia Butcher
Graduate Assistant, Legal Division

SGC:JB:ak

³ A source of income of an official is directly affected by a decision when the source of income initiates, is
a party in, or is the subject of the decision. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)
